blocks.text: How We Size the Outsourcing Ceiling

runs.text: How We Size the Outsourcing Ceiling

blocks.text: In plain terms: the ceiling is the most of a ship's new-construction work that could, in theory, be bought from outside suppliers instead of done inside the prime shipyard — if the only work that truly has to stay in the yard is final assembly, integration, and test. This page explains, from the top, how we put a number on that ceiling, why we had to build it up from two sourced figures rather than read it off a budget line, and what it comes out to.

runs.text: In plain terms:

the ceiling is the most of a ship's new-construction work that could, in theory, be bought from outside suppliers instead of done inside the prime shipyard — if the only work that truly has to stay in the yard is final assembly, integration, and test. This page explains, from the top, how we put a number on that ceiling, why we had to build it up from two sourced figures rather than read it off a budget line, and what it comes out to.

blocks.text: Where this fits in the distributed shipbuilding project

runs.text: Where this fits in the distributed shipbuilding project

blocks.text: The wider project asks one question: how much of the work of building the Navy's warships is moving away from the prime shipyards and out to a distributed base of suppliers — and how big an opening that creates for a new entrant. The work splits in two:

runs.text: The wider project asks one question: how much of the work of building the Navy's warships is moving away from the prime shipyards and out to a distributed base of suppliers — and how big an opening that creates for a new entrant. The work splits in two:

blocks.text: •Looking back — what is already outsourced. A separate Award Analysis workbook reads the supplier records to map the work the shipbuilders already buy from outside: who wins it, how concentrated it is, and when it comes up to be bought again.

runs.text: Looking back — what is already outsourced.
A separate Award Analysis workbook reads the supplier records to map the work the shipbuilders already buy from outside: who wins it, how concentrated it is, and when it comes up to be bought again.

blocks.text: •Looking forward — how much more could move. That is this workbook. It puts an upper bound on outsourcing — the largest share of a ship's build that could, in principle, leave the prime yard.

runs.text: Looking forward — how much more could move.
That is this workbook. It puts an upper bound on outsourcing — the largest share of a ship's build that could, in principle, leave the prime yard.

blocks.text: The two meet at this page's headline read: the opening is the gap between what is distributed today — the share already built away from the prime yard — and the structural ceiling above it. This workbook sizes that ceiling, and so the headroom still on the table.

runs.text: The two meet at this page's headline read: the opening is the gap between what is distributed today — the share already built away from the prime yard — and the structural ceiling above it. This workbook sizes that ceiling, and so the headroom still on the table.

blocks.text: The question in one sentence

runs.text: The question in one sentence

blocks.text: How much higher could outsourcing go? Today a known share of each ship's build already happens away from the prime yard. The question is not what that share is now — it is how high it could climb before it runs into the work that physically cannot leave the yard.

runs.text: How much higher could outsourcing go?

Today a known share of each ship's build already happens away from the prime yard. The question is not what that share is now — it is how high it could climb before it runs into the work that physically cannot leave the yard.

blocks.text: The breakthrough: build the ceiling, don't look it up

runs.text: The breakthrough: build the ceiling, don't look it up

blocks.text: The obvious move would be to find an official figure for “assembly work that must stay” versus “work that could be outsourced.” Two facts forced a different approach:

runs.text: The obvious move would be to find an official figure for “assembly work that must stay” versus “work that could be outsourced.” Two facts forced a different approach:

blocks.text: •The clean line does not exist. The budget lumps the whole hull-and-structure build into one line; it never splits labor from materials, or “assembly” from “parts you could buy elsewhere.” The Pentagon's own budget rules and the shipbuilders' annual reports confirm the split simply is not published.

runs.text: The clean line does not exist.

The budget lumps the whole hull-and-structure build into one line; it never splits labor from materials, or “assembly” from “parts you could buy elsewhere.” The Pentagon's own budget rules and the shipbuilders' annual reports confirm the split simply is not published.

blocks.text: •Today's number is the floor, not the ceiling. The share already built away from the prime yard (Virginia 34%, Columbia 22%, DDG-51 about 13%) is current outsourcing. Using it as the ceiling would assume away the very growth we are trying to size — so it becomes the starting floor, and the ceiling has to come from somewhere else.

runs.text: Today's number is the floor, not the ceiling.

The share already built away from the prime yard (Virginia 34%, Columbia 22%, DDG-51 about 13%) is current outsourcing. Using it as the ceiling would assume away the very growth we are trying to size — so it becomes the starting floor, and the ceiling has to come from somewhere else.

blocks.text: So instead of looking the ceiling up, we build it from two numbers that are published and citable. The rest of this page is those two numbers and the simple arithmetic that turns them into a ceiling.

runs.text: So instead of looking the ceiling up, we
build it
from two numbers that
are
published and citable. The rest of this page is those two numbers and the simple arithmetic that turns them into a ceiling.

blocks.text: Start with the basics: what is “Basic Construction”?

runs.text: Start with the basics: what is “Basic Construction”?

blocks.text: When the Navy buys a ship, the largest piece of the price is a single budget line usually called Basic Construction — the labor and materials to physically build the hull and its structure. It does not include government-furnished gear like the combat system or, on the submarines, the reactor; those are bought separately. Everything in this analysis is measured as a share of that Basic Construction line, because that is the work a shipbuilder can choose to do in-house or send out.

runs.text: When the Navy buys a ship, the largest piece of the price is a single budget line usually called Basic Construction — the labor and materials to physically build the hull and its structure. It does not include government-furnished gear like the combat system or, on the submarines, the reactor; those are bought separately. Everything in this analysis is measured as a share of that Basic Construction line, because that is the work a shipbuilder can choose to do in-house or send out.

blocks.text: The two numbers that do the work

runs.text: The two numbers that do the work

blocks.text: The whole estimate rests on two sourced figures. Both describe the build as shares, so they travel across programs and dollar years:

runs.text: The whole estimate rests on two sourced figures. Both describe the build as shares, so they travel across programs and dollar years:

blocks.text: •How much labor can leave the yard (we call it h). The Navy's submarine program executive has said roughly half the labor hours to build a Virginia-class boat can be done outside the prime yard — the planned ramp from about two million to five million supplier hours a year. So about 50% of the labor hours are movable.

runs.text: How much labor can leave the yard (we call it h).

The Navy's submarine program executive has said roughly half the labor hours to build a Virginia-class boat can be done outside the prime yard — the planned ramp from about two million to five million supplier hours a year. So about 50% of the labor hours are movable.

blocks.text: •How much of the build is labor in the first place (we call it L). Congressional Research Service testimony puts shipyard labor at roughly 40% of a ship's total cost, and an independent cost index puts it in the same 40-to-48% range. Because that 40% is measured against the ship's whole price — including the government-furnished gear — labor's share of just the Basic Construction line is higher, so we use about 50% for the submarines and 45% for the destroyer.

runs.text: How much of the build is labor in the first place (we call it L).

Congressional Research Service testimony puts shipyard labor at roughly 40% of a ship's total cost, and an independent cost index puts it in the same 40-to-48% range. Because that 40% is measured against the ship's whole price — including the government-furnished gear — labor's share of just the Basic Construction line is higher, so we use about 50% for the submarines and 45% for the destroyer.

blocks.text: Why this matters: the submarine half-the-hours figure already implies today's roughly 20% of hours is on its way to about 50% — a jump of about two-and-a-half times in the labor that can leave the yard.

runs.text: Why this matters:
the submarine half-the-hours figure already implies today's roughly 20% of hours is on its way to about 50% — a jump of about two-and-a-half times in the labor that can leave the yard.

blocks.text: The idea in one line: core vs. ceiling

runs.text: The idea in one line: core vs. ceiling

blocks.text: Picture the Basic Construction build as a whole. Part of it is work that has to stay in the prime yard no matter what — final assembly, integration, and test. Call that the core. The ceiling is simply everything that is not core:

runs.text: Picture the Basic Construction build as a whole. Part of it is work that has to stay in the prime yard no matter what — final assembly, integration, and test. Call that the core. The ceiling is simply everything that is not core:

blocks.text: •Core = the labor share that stays put = labor share × the part of labor that cannot leave.

runs.text: Core
= the labor share that stays put =
labor share × the part of labor that cannot leave

blocks.text: •Ceiling = everything else = 100% minus the core.

runs.text: Ceiling
= everything else =
100% minus the core

blocks.text: That is the entire model. Two sourced numbers go in; a core and a ceiling come out, for each ship class and for the portfolio as a whole.

runs.text: That is the entire model. Two sourced numbers go in; a core and a ceiling come out, for each ship class and for the portfolio as a whole.

blocks.text: From hours to dollars

runs.text: From hours to dollars

blocks.text: The two numbers above are about hours, but the answer people want is in dollars. The bridge adds the materials side back in. Outsourced dollars are the sum of two flows:

runs.text: The two numbers above are about hours, but the answer people want is in dollars. The bridge adds the materials side back in. Outsourced dollars are the sum of two flows:

blocks.text: 1.The prime-yard labor that relocates to suppliers — the movable hours turned into dollars.

runs.text: The prime-yard labor that relocates to suppliers — the movable hours turned into dollars.

blocks.text: 2.The parts and materials bought from outside — a share of the non-labor half of the build, set by how much of that material is genuinely up for grabs rather than locked to a single source.

runs.text: The parts and materials bought from outside — a share of the non-labor half of the build, set by how much of that material is genuinely up for grabs rather than locked to a single source.

blocks.text: Turning the second dial up or down is what moves the answer between a labor-only reading (about a quarter of the build) and the full ceiling. For sizing a supplier opportunity — where bought-in material is part of the prize — that dial sits high.

runs.text: Turning the second dial up or down is what moves the answer between a labor-only reading (about a quarter of the build) and the full ceiling. For sizing a supplier opportunity — where bought-in material is part of the prize — that dial sits high.

blocks.text: What we found

runs.text: What we found

blocks.text: Run the two sourced numbers through the model and the ceiling lands near three-quarters of Basic Construction on every program — roughly three times today's outsourced share:

runs.text: Run the two sourced numbers through the model and the ceiling lands near three-quarters of Basic Construction on every program — roughly three times today's outsourced share:

blocks.text: Table 1. The ceiling read at a glance

runs.text: Table 1. The ceiling read at a glance

blocks.text: The punchline: the nuclear submarines carry the bigger must-stay core (25% vs. the destroyer's 20%) — the reactor work keeps more locked inside the yard — yet even they could roughly double or triple what they outsource today.

runs.text: The punchline:

the nuclear submarines carry the bigger must-stay core (25% vs. the destroyer's 20%) — the reactor work keeps more locked inside the yard — yet even they could roughly double or triple what they outsource today.

blocks.text: Two honest limits

runs.text: Two honest limits

blocks.text: •The destroyer's two numbers are analyst judgment.

runs.text: The destroyer's two numbers are analyst judgment.

blocks.text: ◦There is no surface-ship equivalent of the submarine half-the-hours figure, so we set the destroyer's movable-hours and labor shares by reasoning (no reactor, so a smaller locked core) rather than a direct quote. They are meant to be adjusted.

runs.text: There is no surface-ship equivalent of the submarine half-the-hours figure, so we set the destroyer's movable-hours and labor shares by reasoning (no reactor, so a smaller locked core) rather than a direct quote. They are meant to be adjusted.

blocks.text: •The 40% labor figure is of the whole ship, not just the build.

runs.text: The 40% labor figure is of the whole ship, not just the build.

blocks.text: ◦Re-basing it onto just the Basic Construction line is a judgment call (we used about 50% for subs, 45% for the destroyer), so the workbook lets you sweep it.

runs.text: Re-basing it onto just the Basic Construction line is a judgment call (we used about 50% for subs, 45% for the destroyer), so the workbook lets you sweep it.

blocks.text: •The dollars are illustrative; the shares are the answer.

runs.text: The dollars are illustrative; the shares are the answer.

blocks.text: ◦Figures are in as-reported (then-year) dollars to show magnitude. The ratios — core, ceiling, and the multiple over today — are what the analysis stands on, and they do not depend on the dollar year.

runs.text: Figures are in as-reported (then-year) dollars to show magnitude. The ratios — core, ceiling, and the multiple over today — are what the analysis stands on, and they do not depend on the dollar year.
